items.foco: Respiratory syncytial virus as the main agent
items.title: RSV as the main agent in bronchiolitis and croup
items.contentHtml:
Respiratory syncytial virus as the main agent

  • The respiratory syncytial virus is the main etiological agent of bronchiolitis and a frequent cause of viral croup in infants.
  • It is an RNA virus of the Paramyxoviridae family, which produces cellular syncytia and necrosis of the respiratory epithelium.
  • Transmission is by direct contact with secretions or fomites; the seasonal peak in temperate climates occurs between autumn and winter.
  • Respiratory syncytial virus infection induces partial immunity, so reinfections are possible, although generally milder.
  • Certain diagnosis is made by direct immunofluorescence or polymerase chain reaction in nasopharyngeal aspirate.

items.foco: Bronchiolitis: typical age less than 2 years
items.title: Typical age in bronchiolitis
items.contentHtml:
Bronchiolitis: typical age under 2 years

  • Bronchiolitis typically affects children under 2 years of age, with peak incidence between 2 and 6 months of age.
  • Age under 12 weeks is an independent risk factor for severe disease and hospitalization.
  • In neonates and small infants, the immaturity of the immune system and the smaller lumen of the airways airways favor bronchiolar obstruction.
  • The clinical presentation includes rhinorrhea, cough, tachypnea, wheezing and signs of respiratory difficulty such as nasal flaring and drawing.
  • The diagnosis is clinical; Routine imaging is not required, although chest x-ray may show hyperinflation and atelectasis.

items.foco: Croup: inspiratory stridor and barking cough
items.title: Croup: stridor and barking cough
items.contentHtml:
Crup: inspiratory stridor and barking cough

  • Crup is characterized by inspiratory stridor and barking cough (metallic cough, similar to a seal's bark), due to subglottic edema.
  • It is more common between 6 months and 3 years, with a predominance in boys and in autumn-winter.
  • Airway obstruction Superior produces a pattern of respiratory difficulty with suprasternal and intercostal redrawing.
  • Severity is evaluated with the Westley scale, which scores stridor, redrawing, air entry, cyanosis and consciousness; a score >7 indicates severe croup.
  • The diagnosis is clinical; The anteroposterior neck x-ray may show the rook sign (subglottic pencil point narrowing).

items.foco: Viral vs. Croup Haemophilus influenzae epiglottitis
items.title: Differential diagnosis: viral croup vs. epiglottitis
items.contentHtml:
Viral croup vs. epiglottitis due to Haemophilus influenzae

  • Viral croup has gradual onset, low fever, barking cough and inspiratory stridor; It improves with corticosteroids.
  • Epiglottitis due to Haemophilus influenzae type b is acute in onset, with high fever, dysphagia, drooling and a tripod position, without barking cough.
  • In epiglottitis, the stridor is inspiratory and the voice is muffled; The lateral neck x-ray shows the thumb sign (edematous epiglottis).
  • Epiglottitis requires urgent airway management (intubation or tracheostomy) and antibiotics against Haemophilus influenzae; Never perform an examination with a tongue depressor due to the risk of complete obstruction.
  • Vaccination against Haemophilus influenzae type b has drastically reduced the incidence of bacterial epiglottitis.

items.foco: Corticosteroids in croup: oral or intramuscular dexamethasone
items.title: Corticosteroids in croup: dexamethasone
items.contentHtml:
Corticosteroids in croup: oral or intramuscular dexamethasone

  • The treatment of choice for croup is dexamethasone in a single dose of 0.15 to 0.6 mg/kg (maximum 10 mg) orally or intramuscularly.
  • dexamethasone reduces subglottic edema after 2-4 hours, reducing the hospitalization rate and the need for nebulized adrenaline.
  • The oral route is preferable due to its bioavailability equivalent to the intramuscular route and less pain; in vomiting, intramuscular is used.
  • In mild to moderate croup (Westley scale <7), dexamethasone alone is usually sufficient; in severe cases it is combined with nebulized adrenaline.
  • Inhaled corticosteroids (budesonide) are not recommended as first line due to inferior evidence to systemic dexamethasone.

items.title: Nebulized adrenaline in severe croup
items.contentHtml:
Nebulized adrenaline in severe croup

  • In severe croup (Westley scale >7 or stridor at rest with marked indrawing) nebulized adrenaline (L-epinephrine) is indicated at 1:1000, dose of 0.5 mL/kg (maximum 5 mL) diluted in saline solution, administered with mask.
  • Nebulized adrenaline produces local vasoconstriction and reduces subglottic edema in 10-30 minutes, with a transient effect of 1-2 hours.
  • It can be repeated every 20-30 minutes if severity persists; The patient should be monitored for possible rebound effect.
  • If there is no response to two doses of nebulized adrenaline and corticosteroids, evaluate the need for endotracheal intubation.
  • The combination of nebulized adrenaline with dexamethasone is the standard in severe croup; Do not use racemic epinephrine as a substitute if it is not available.

items.foco: Oxygen and aspiration of secretions in bronchiolitis
items.title: Oxygen and aspiration in bronchiolitis
items.contentHtml:
Oxygen and aspiration of secretions in bronchiolitis

  • Oxygen therapy is indicated if oxygen saturation by pulse oximetry is persistently <90% (or <92% according to local guidelines).
  • Wet oxygen is administered by nasal cannula or helmet; the objective is to maintain saturation >92-94%.
  • Aspiration of nasopharyngeal secretions is indicated when there is significant nasal obstruction that makes feeding or breathing difficult, especially in infants <3 months.
  • Aspiration of secretions should be gentle and not routine, limited to the upper airway to avoid trauma and bronchospasm.
  • No use respiratory physiotherapy (vibration, percussion) in acute bronchiolitis; there is no evidence of benefit and it may worsen the obstruction.

items.title: Hospitalization criteria in bronchiolitis
items.contentHtml:
Hospitalization criteria in bronchiolitis

  • Hospitalization criteria include: oxygen saturation <90-92% on room air, age <3 months, history of prematurity (<34 weeks), comorbidities (congenital heart disease, bronchopulmonary dysplasia, immunodeficiency).
  • Signs of moderate-severe respiratory distress: tachypnea >60 breaths/min, nasal flaring, intercostal or suprasternal indrawing, cyanosis, apneas.
  • Inability to feed orally (intake <50% of usual) or dehydration.
  • Lack of response to outpatient management or clinical deterioration in observation.
  • Hospitalization allows monitoring, oxygen therapy, aspiration of secretions and nutritional support (nasogastric or intravenous tube).

items.foco: Do not use routine bronchodilators in mild bronchiolitis
items.title: Non-routine bronchodilators in mild bronchiolitis
items.contentHtml:
Do not use routine bronchodilators in mild bronchiolitis

  • In mild bronchiolitis (without significant respiratory distress, saturation >92%) the routine use of bronchodilators such as salbutamol or nebulized adrenaline is not recommended.
  • Clinical trials show that bronchodilators do not reduce the rate of hospitalization or shorten the length of stay in mild to mild bronchiolitis. moderate.
  • In cases of suspected bronchial hyperreactivity or recurrent wheezing, a therapeutic trial with inhaled salbutamol can be performed; If there is no objective improvement (Wood-Downes-Ferrés scale), it is suspended.
  • Nebulized adrenaline is also not routinely indicated in bronchiolitis; Its use is limited to severe episodes with severe respiratory difficulty.
  • The mainstay of management in mild bronchiolitis is support (hydration, aspiration of secretions if necessary) and monitoring for warning signs.

items.foco: Palivizumab in prophylaxis for RSV bronchiolitis
items.title: Prophylactic palivizumab against RSV
items.contentHtml:
Palivizumab in prophylaxis for RSV bronchiolitis

  • Palivizumab is a humanized monoclonal antibody against respiratory syncytial virus, indicated in prophylaxis for high-risk infants.
  • The dose is 15 mg/kg intramuscularly once a month during the respiratory syncytial virus season (generally 5 dose).
  • Main indications: premature <29 weeks gestation at the beginning of the season, bronchopulmonary dysplasia requiring oxygen in the last 6 months, hemodynamically significant congenital heart disease.
  • It is not indicated in healthy term infants or as a treatment for established bronchiolitis.
  • Efficacy reduces hospitalization rate for respiratory syncytial virus at 45-55%; It does not prevent infection, only serious illness.

items.foco: Spasmodic croup: recurrent and without fever
items.title: Recurrent spasmodic croup without fever
items.contentHtml:
Spasmodic croup: recurrent and without fever

  • Spasmodic croup (recurrent croup) typically occurs in children aged 1 to 3 years, with sudden episodes of inspiratory stridor and barking cough that appear at night, without fever or catarrhal prodromes.
  • It is believed to be of allergic origin or due to hyperreactivity. laryngeal, often triggered by gastroesophageal reflux or environmental irritants.
  • The absence of fever and recurrence (more than 2 episodes) are key to distinguish it from typical viral croup.
  • Acute management is similar to viral croup: oral dexamethasone 0.15-0.6 mg/kg and, if severe, adrenaline nebulized.
  • To prevent recurrences, it is recommended to treat gastroesophageal reflux if it is present; antihistamines do not have solid evidence.

items.foco: Campbell's sign in croup: suprasternal indrawing
items.title: Campbell's sign in croup
items.contentHtml:
Campbell's sign in croup: suprasternal indrawing

  • The Campbell sign refers to the suprasternal indrawing visible during inspiration, indicative of obstruction of the upper airway, characteristic of croup.
  • It is produced by the contraction of the accessory muscles (sternocleidomastoid, scalenes) by generating negative inspiratory pressure against a subglottic resistance.
  • The presence of Campbell's sign together with inspiratory stridor and barking cough supports the clinical diagnosis of croup.
  • The intensity of suprasternal indrawing correlates with the severity of the obstruction and is included in the Westley scale (score 0-2 according to absence, mild or severe).
  • In the differential diagnosis, suprasternal indrawing can also be seen in epiglottitis, but accompanied by drooling and dysphagia, without barking cough.
